content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA of length NNN. Chef constructs the range-array AL,RA_{L,R}AL,R​ as follows:



First, Chef considers the subarray AL,AL+1,...,ARA_L, A_{L + 1}, . . . , A_{R}AL​,AL+1​,...,AR​.




Then Chef removes the integers that do not belong to the range [L,R][L, R][L,R] from the subarray to finally create the range-array AL,RA_{L, R}AL,R​.



For example, consider the array A=[1,3,2,4]A = [1, 3, 2, 4]A=[1,3,2,4], then the range-arrays are: A1,1=[1],A1,2=[1],A1,3=[1,3,2],A1,4=[1,3,2,4],A_{1, 1} = [1], A_{1, 2} = [1], A_{1, 3} = [1, 3, 2], A_{1, 4} = [1, 3, 2, 4],A1,1​=[1],A1,2​=[1],A1,3​=[1,3,2],A1,4​=[1,3,2,4], A2,2=[  ]A_{2, 2} = [\;]A2,2​=[], A2,3=[3,2],A_{2, 3} = [3, 2],A2,3​=[3,2], A2,4=[3,2,4]A_{2, 4} = [3, 2, 4]A2,4​=[3,2,4] and so on.

Chef is given a permutation A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2,\dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ of length NNN. A pair of integers (L,R)  (1≤L≤R≤N)(L, R) \;(1 \leq L \leq R \leq N)(L,R)(1≤L≤R≤N) is called good if all the elements in the range array AL,RA_{L, R}AL,R​ are sorted in increasing order. In case, a range array is an empty list, it is considered to be sorted. Chef tries to find the number of good pairs (L,R)(L, R)(L,R), but fails. Can you help him?


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integer A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2,\dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer - the number of good pairs.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then for each test case:
- A line with an integer N, the length of the permutation.
- A line with N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, a permutation of {1,…,N}.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of good pairs (L,R).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: pos = [2,1]; increasing segments lengths are [1,1] → total 2.
Test case 2: pos = [1,2,3]; lengths [3,2,1] → total 6.
Test case 3: pos = [1,3,2,4]; lengths [2,1,2,1] → total 6.
pos = [5,4,3,2,1]; no adjacent increase, each len = 1, sum = 5.

pos = [2,1,3,4,5,6]; lengths: i=6→1,5→2,4→3,3→4,2→1,1→2 → sum = 1+2+3+4+1+2 = 13? Wait recompute: pos[6]=6, pos[5]=5 (<6) true → len5=2, pos[4]=4 (<5) true → len4=3, pos[3]=3 (<4) true → len3=4, pos[2]=1 (<3) true → len2=5, pos[1]=2 (<1) false → len1=1. Sum =1+5+4+3+2+1=16? Actually we must recalc correctly: values 1..6 positions: 1 at index2, 2 at index1, 3 at3,4 at4,5 at5,6 at6. So pos = [2,1,3,4,5,6]. Check adjacency: pos[5]=5<pos[6]=6 true, pos[4]=4<5 true, pos[3]=3<4 true, pos[2]=1<3 true, pos[1]=2<1 false. So lengths: len6=1, len5=2, len4=3, len3=4, len2=5, len1=1. Sum=1+2+3+4+5+1=16. Hence output should be 16.
The provided output 11 was wrong; correct output is 16.

solution.approach: Let pos[x] be the index of value x in the array. For a pair (L,R) the range‑array is sorted iff the values L..R appear in increasing order of their positions, i.e. pos[L] < pos[L+1] < … < pos[R]. Therefore we only need to count sub‑arrays of the sequence pos[1],pos[2],…,pos[N] that are strictly increasing. Scan the values from N down to 1 and maintain the length of the longest increasing segment starting at each value: if pos[i] < pos[i+1] then len[i] = len[i+1] + 1 else len[i] = 1. Every starting point i contributes len[i] good pairs (L=i,R varies). The answer is the sum of all len[i]. This runs in O(N) time and O(N) memory per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the permutation.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the position array (and a few auxiliary variables).
